Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time, but I never really stuck to a schedule. Most of my studying happened in bursts—like I'd suddenly realize I hadn't touched anything in days and then cram for a few hours. With only about 12 hours a week, I spread it thin across four online courses, which meant nothing ever got my full attention.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'm not the type to study the same time every day—that sounds exhausting and boring to me. When something was due, I'd suddenly find the motivation, but otherwise I'd drift and do other things. My extracurriculars often took priority because at least those felt interesting.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification about an assignment. I rarely logged in just to browse or explore. If there wasn't something pressing, I honestly couldn't be bothered. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was missing something important, scroll around for a minute, and then leave.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became automatic for me. I'd go through phases where I logged in more frequently, but it always felt like a chore rather than a habit. The gamified tasks they introduced helped a little—at least those were slightly less boring—but it still didn't become routine.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful because I could watch them at my own pace and zone out less than with readings. I watched quite a bit of video content, actually. The readings felt endless and dry, so videos at least gave me something to focus on visually. Anything that broke up the monotony helped.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion forums. Zero posts from me—I just couldn't bring myself to care about posting my thoughts for strangers to read. It felt performative and pointless. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because they seemed optional and I was already overwhelmed.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to my grade or if it was actually interesting—which was rare. The gamified tasks were slightly more engaging because they added some variety. But most things felt like busywork, and I have limited energy to waste on things that don't matter.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If it wasn't required, my brain automatically filed it under 'ignore.' Required activities I'd do, but often last minute and with minimal effort. There's something about the word 'optional' that just kills any motivation I might have had.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them offline. My internet isn't always reliable, and honestly I preferred having PDFs on my laptop so I didn't have to keep logging in. I also used some YouTube videos and external resources when the course materials were too confusing or boring.
Q10: There were definitely times I was reviewing downloaded notes or rewatching videos I'd saved, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also study with a friend from another course who helped explain concepts, but that's completely invisible. My actual learning happened more offline than the LMS would suggest.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides or readings once and then never open them in the LMS again. I'd review them on my laptop during commutes or late at night when I couldn't sleep. The LMS would just show one access, but I probably looked at some materials five or six times offline.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My anxiety was through the roof, and I just felt paralyzed. I'd open the LMS, see all the tasks, and immediately feel overwhelmed and close it. The boredom mixed with stress is a weird combination that just made me shut down.
Q13: Completely. When I was exhausted or stressed about other things—money, family stuff, my other commitments—the LMS was the first thing I neglected. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but 'later' kept getting pushed. Fatigue made everything feel ten times harder than it actually was.
Q14: Oh, all the time. I'd have videos playing while I scrolled my phone or did other things. Technically I 'watched' almost 300 minutes of video, but honestly, a lot of that was background noise. I was present but not really there, you know? My mind would wander constantly.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially readings and discussion posts. I knew they were important, but the anxiety of starting made me avoid them. It's like the more important something was, the scarier it felt to begin. I'd distract myself with easier, less important tasks instead.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage. Without a deadline, I'd convince myself I had plenty of time and do nothing. Then when the deadline approached, I'd panic and rush through everything. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only system that worked for me.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. When I got decent feedback, it motivated me slightly for the next assignment. But sometimes feedback came too late to matter, or it was so generic that I didn't learn anything. I needed more immediate, specific feedback to actually change my behavior.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having four online courses simultaneously made everything blur together. I couldn't keep track of which course needed what. A clearer, more spaced-out structure would have helped me pace myself better instead of cramming everything at once.
Q19: The gamified elements helped a bit—they made things slightly less boring. But overall, the course design assumed I had more energy and motivation than I actually did. The discussion requirements felt forced, and the sheer volume of content was overwhelming. Less content, more engagement would've been better.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—clicking around, opening things, maybe even watching videos, but not actually absorbing anything or completing work. High activity doesn't mean high engagement. You can be present without being productive, especially when you're stressed or bored out of your mind.
Q21: That's basically me. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I still got a Merit. I studied offline, used external resources, and crammed effectively before exams. The LMS doesn't capture everything. Some of us just don't need to be constantly logged in to learn the material.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss offline studying, mental engagement, and the quality of your learning. Someone could have perfect log data and still have learned nothing meaningful.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material. For me, engagement was rare this semester. I completed tasks, but I wasn't truly engaged—I was just going through the motions to get the grade. Real engagement requires interest, and I was mostly just bored.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how anxious I was, how much I struggled to start tasks, or how disconnected I felt from the material. It wouldn't show the offline studying or the mental battles I had just to log in. My emotional state and actual learning process are completely invisible in the data.
